text: Combined strength training methods (two or more of high load, submaximal load, and plyometric training) produce the largest improvement in running performance for middle- and long-distance runners.
population: middle-distance and long-distance runners (moderately to highly trained)

text: High load strength training (≥80% 1RM) improves running performance (time trial and time to exhaustion) in middle- and long-distance runners.
population: middle-distance and long-distance runners (moderately to highly trained)

text: Plyometric training alone does not significantly improve running performance in middle- and long-distance runners.
population: middle-distance and long-distance runners (moderately to highly trained)

text: Strength training methods (high load, submaximal, plyometric, or combined) do not improve VO2max, velocity at VO2max, maximum metabolic steady state, or sprint capacity in runners.
population: middle-distance and long-distance runners (moderately to highly trained)

text: The performance benefits of strength training for runners appear to operate through neuromuscular mechanisms (e.g., running economy) rather than through improvements in aerobic capacity or sprint ability.
Strength training programs of 6-40 weeks duration with 1-4 sessions per week are effective for improving running performance when using high loads or combined methods.
population: middle-distance and long-distance runners (moderately to highly trained)

text: Subject characteristics (sex, age, body mass, height, VO2max, performance level, strength training experience) and intervention parameters (weeks, sessions per week, total sessions) do not moderate the effect of strength training on running performance.
population: middle-distance and long-distance runners (moderately to highly trained)
